## Summary
Could two individuals be functionally identical yet have systematically different subjective experiences (e.g., your red looks like my green)? The inverted qualia thought experiment challenges functionalist theories by suggesting that functional organization underdetermines phenomenal character.

## Evidence
Arguments for the conceivability of qualia inversion (Shoemaker, Block) rely on the logical separability of functional roles and phenomenal properties. Empirical constraints from color science show that the asymmetries of color space (unique hues, warm-cool distinction) may make systematic inversion detectable, though not all philosophers accept this argument.

## Significance
The inverted qualia problem is central to the debate between functionalism and qualia realism. If inversion is genuinely possible, it shows that no amount of behavioral or functional data can fully characterize conscious experience, with implications for AI consciousness assessments.
